1: ʾāmartî ʾeśmĕrâ dĕrākay mēḥăṭōʾ bilĕšônî ʾeśmĕrâ lĕpî mazzōm (ʾāmartî ʾeśmĕrâ ḏĕrāḵay mēḥăṭōʾ ḇilĕšônî ʾeśmĕrâ lĕp̄î mazzōm): 'I said, 'I will guard my ways, that I may not sin with my tongue; I will guard my mouth with a muzzle.'' Psalm 39 is attributed to David with a dedication to Jeduthun, one of the chief temple musicians (1 Chr 16:41-42). The opening resolve — ʾeśmĕrâ ('I will guard') applied to both darkay ('my ways') and pî ('my mouth') — describes the determined self-restraint of someone who has learned that careless speech about God's governance leads to sin. The noun mazzôm (or miśmôr, 'muzzle, bridle') is uniquely striking: the mouth is to be as controlled as a bridled horse. The reason: bĕʿôd rāšāʿ lĕnegdî, 'while the wicked are in my presence' — the psalmist restrains speech about divine justice in the hearing of the ungodly, lest his complaint be ammunition for their atheism.
2: neʾĕlamtî dûmiyyâ heḥĕšêtî mîṭṭôb ûmaʿkōbî neʿkār (neʾĕlamtî ḏûmiyyâ heḥĕšêtî mîṭṭôb ûmaʿkōḇî neʿkār): 'I was mute and silent; I held my peace to no avail, and my distress grew worse.' The three verbs of silence — neʾĕlamtî ('I was made mute'), dûmiyyâ ('I was still/silent'), heḥĕšêtî ('I kept quiet') — escalate: silence was attempted at every level. The phrase mîṭṭôb ('from good') suggests the silence extended even to good things — he held back not just complaints but anything. The result: ûmaʿkōbî neʿkār, 'and my pain was stirred/increased.' The silence that was meant to manage distress instead intensified it: suppressed anguish becomes more acute. The psalm's opening explains why speech eventually erupts (v.3).
3: ḥam-libbî bĕqirbî bĕhaggîgî tîbʿar-ʾēš dibbartî billĕšônî (ḥam-libbî bĕqirḇî bĕhaggîgî ṯîḇʿar-ʾēš dibbartî ḇillĕšônî): 'My heart became hot within me. As I mused, the fire burned; then I spoke with my tongue.' The heart's heat — ḥam-libbî ('my heart became hot') — describes suppressed anguish building to overflow. The verb hāgâ (bĕhaggîgî, 'as I mused/meditated') is the meditation root of Ps 1:2 — but here meditation intensifies rather than calms: tîbʿar-ʾēš, 'the fire burned.' Fire is the image of contained energy suddenly beyond control: the discipline of vv.1-2 fails, and dibbartî billĕšônî ('I spoke with my tongue') — the resolution breaks. The psalmist speaks — and what he speaks is the meditation on mortality in vv.4-6.
4: hôdîʿēnî YHWH qiṣṣî ûmiddat yāmay mah-hîʾ ʾēdĕʿâ meh-ḥādēl ʾānî (hôḏîʿēnî YHWH qiṣṣî ûmiḏḏaṯ yāmay mah-hîʾ ʾēḏĕʿâ meh-ḥāḏēl ʾānî): 'O LORD, make me know my end and what is the measure of my days; let me know how fleeting I am!' The prayer for self-knowledge hôdîʿēnî ('make me know!') is unusual: the psalmist asks YHWH to reveal his mortality — qiṣṣî ('my end'), middat yāmay ('the measure of my days'). The phrase meh-ḥādēl ʾānî, 'how transient/failing I am,' uses ḥādal ('to cease, fail, stop') — the psalmist as someone who will simply stop. The prayer for mortality-awareness is not morbid self-pity but a request for the theological insight that Ps 90:12 calls wisdom: 'teach us to number our days.'
5: hinnēh ṭĕpāḥôt nātattâ yāmay wĕḥeldî kĕʾayin nĕgdekā (hinnēh ṭĕp̄āḥôṯ nāṯattâ yāmay wĕḥeldî kĕʾayin neḡḏeḵā): 'Behold, you have made my days a few handbreadths, and my lifetime is as nothing before you. Surely all mankind stands as a mere breath!' The handbreadth (ṭepaḥ) — roughly 3 inches, the smallest unit of measurement — reduces the span of a life to a trivial interval. The phrase wĕḥeldî kĕʾayin nĕgdekā, 'my lifespan is as nothing before you,' uses the comparison to divine eternity: sub specie aeternitatis, any human life is negligible in duration. The conclusion: ʾak kol-hebel kol-ʾādām niṣṣāb selah, 'surely every man standing is but breath.' The word hebel ('breath, vapor, vanity') — the central term of Ecclesiastes — makes human existence as transient as an exhaled breath. The Selah invites meditation on the weight of this reality.
6: ʾak bĕṣelem yithallak-ʾîš ʾak-hebel yehĕmāyûn yiṣbōr wĕlōʾ-yēdaʿ mî-yōsĕpēm (ʾaḵ bĕṣelem yiṯhallak-ʾîš ʾaḵ-heḇel yehĕmāyûn yiṣḇōr wĕlōʾ-yēḏaʿ mî-yōsĕp̄ēm): 'Surely a man goes about like a shadow! Surely for nothing they are in turmoil; man heaps up wealth and does not know who will gather.' The phrase bĕṣelem yithallak-ʾîš ('a man goes about like a shadow/image') uses ṣelem ('image, shadow') — the same word as 'image of God' (bĕṣelem ʾĕlōhîm, Gen 1:26-27) — here ironically: humanity made in the image is itself a shadow. The turmoil of wealth-accumulation: yiṣbōr wĕlōʾ-yēdaʿ mî-yōsĕpēm, 'he piles up and does not know who will gather it.' Jesus's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) directly engages this wisdom.
7: wĕʿattâ mah-qiwwîtî ʾădōnāy tôḥaltî lĕkā hîʾ (wĕʿattâ mah-qiwwîtî ʾăḏōnāy tôḥaltî lĕḵā hîʾ): 'And now, O Lord, for what do I wait? My hope is in you.' The transitional wĕʿattâ ('and now') marks the pivot from the meditation on mortality (vv.4-6) to its theological conclusion: if human life is hebel and human wealth is uncertain, what can the psalmist wait for? The answer: tôḥaltî lĕkā hîʾ ('my hope is in you — it is you'). The emphatic hîʾ ('it is') makes YHWH the exclusive content of hope: not health, not prosperity, not longevity — YHWH himself. The tôḥalt root (from yāḥal, 'wait/hope') identifies the type of hope: taut, expectant, rope-like — a hope that pulls toward its object.
8: mikkol-pĕšāʿay haṣṣîlēnî ḥerpat nābāl ʾal-tĕśîmēnî (mikḵol-p̄ĕšāʿay haṣṣîlēnî ḥerpat nāḇāl ʾal-tĕśîmēnî): 'Deliver me from all my transgressions. Do not make me the scorn of the fool!' The two petitions: haṣṣîlēnî ('deliver me') from pĕšāʿay ('my transgressions/rebellions') — deliverance from sin's consequences. And: ʾal-tĕśîmēnî ḥerpat nābāl, 'do not make me an object of reproach for the fool' — the nābāl ('moral fool' of Ps 14:1) who denies God's governance would use the psalmist's suffering as evidence that righteousness does not pay. The prayer against becoming fodder for practical atheism joins personal deliverance to theological witness.
9: neʾĕlamtî lōʾ ʾeptaḥ pî kî ʾattâ ʿāśîtā (neʾĕlamtî lōʾ ʾep̄taḥ pî kî ʾattâ ʿāśîṯā): 'I am mute; I do not open my mouth, for it is you who have done it.' The silence of vv.1-2 returns: neʾĕlamtî lōʾ ʾeptaḥ pî ('I am mute, I do not open my mouth'). But now the reason is given: kî ʾattâ ʿāśîtā, 'for you have done it.' The suffering is acknowledged as divine action — the same theology as Ps 38:2-3 but now stated simply and without accusation. The psalmist does not complain to others about what God has done; silence before humans is the response to the recognition that divine sovereignty governs all that happens. This is the silent submission of Job 1:21 ('the LORD gave, the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD').
10: hāsēr mēʿālay nigʿekā mittignûrat yādĕkā ʾănî kālîtî (hāsēr mēʿālay niḡʿeḵā mittignûraṯ yāḏĕḵā ʾănî ḵālîṯî): 'Remove your stroke from me; I am spent by the hostility of your hand.' The imperative hāsēr ('remove, turn away!') from sûr applied to nigʿekā ('your blow/stroke') — the divine affliction of v.2 that is experienced as YHWH's hand. The phrase mittignûrat yādĕkā ('from the hostility/contention of your hand') — tigra' may mean 'blow/combat/opposition' — pictures the divine hand pressing down. The result: ʾănî kālîtî, 'I am spent/consumed' — from kālâ ('to be finished, consumed'). The psalmist is at the limit of his capacity to bear the divine correction.
11: bĕtôkāḥôt ʿal-ʿāwōn yissartā ʾîš wattemes kaʿāš ḥămûdô ʾak hebel kol-hāʾādām selah (bĕṯôḵāḥôṯ ʿal-ʿāwōn yissartā ʾîš wattemes kaʿāš ḥămûdô ʾaḵ heḇel ḵol-hāʾāḏām selah): 'When you discipline a man with rebukes for sin, you consume like a moth what is dear to him; surely all mankind is a mere breath!' The metaphor of moth consumption — kāʿāš ḥămûdô ('like a moth, what is dear to him') — images the silent, invisible destruction of what is most precious: the moth consumes from within, leaving the outward form intact but the substance eaten away. Divine discipline for sin operates similarly: gradually consuming the things one holds dear. The Selah again punctuates the conclusion ʾak hebel kol-hāʾādām, 'surely all humanity is a breath' — reinforcing the transience established in v.5.
12: šimʿâ tĕpillātî YHWH wĕšawʿātî haʾăzînâ ʾel-dimʿātî ʾal-teḥeraš (šimʿâ ṯĕp̄illāṯî YHWH wĕšawʿāṯî haʾăzînâ ʾel-dimʿāṯî ʾal-teḥeraš): 'Hear my prayer, O LORD, and give ear to my cry; hold not your peace at my tears.' The petition summons three divine senses: hearing for the prayer (tĕpillâ), attentive listening for the cry (šawʿâ), and responding to the tears (dimʿâ). The phrase ʾal-teḥeraš ('do not be silent!') makes the tears themselves a form of prayer — even inarticulate weeping demands divine response. The theological ground: kî gēr ʾānōkî ʿimmĕkā tôšāb kĕkol-ʾăbōtāy, 'for I am a sojourner with you, a guest, as all my fathers were.' The psalmist's identity as a gēr ('sojourner, alien') before YHWH provides the basis for his appeal — sojourners depend entirely on their host's hospitality (cf. Heb 11:13: 'strangers and pilgrims on the earth').
13: hāšaʿ mimmennî wĕʾabligâ bĕṭerem ʾēlēk wĕʾênennî (hāšaʿ mimmennî wĕʾaḇliḡâ ḇĕṭerem ʾēlēk wĕʾênennî): 'Look away from me, that I may smile again, before I depart and am no more!' The closing request is striking: hāšaʿ mimmennî ('look away from me!') — previously the psalmist prayed for God to look at him (v.12); now he prays for divine gaze to relent. The divine attention has been experienced as the source of his affliction (v.10-11); a momentary lifting of that gaze would allow recovery. The purpose wĕʾabligâ ('that I might brighten/recover a smile') from bālag ('to smile, brighten') — even a brief respite before the end. The phrase bĕṭerem ʾēlēk wĕʾênennî, 'before I go and am no more,' is the psalm's most poignant phrase — the awareness of mortality pressing close. The psalm ends not with resolution but with the raw prayer of one who expects death soon and wants a moment's relief before it comes.
